inline_review:
commit b00dc5b6b92ea542b5dfb95b12596043ae68b969
Author: Leno Hou <lenohou@gmail.com>
Subject: mm/mglru: fix cgroup OOM during MGLRU state switching

This commit introduces an intermediate lru_drain_core state during MGLRU
state transitions to mitigate cgroup OOMs. While in this draining state, the
reclaimer attempts to scan both MGLRU and traditional LRU lists sequentially
to ensure folios are visible to at least one reclaim path.

> diff --git a/mm/vmscan.c b/mm/vmscan.c
> index 33287ba4a500..88b9db06e331 100644
> --- a/mm/vmscan.c
> +++ b/mm/vmscan.c
> @@ -886,7 +886,7 @@ static enum folio_references folio_check_references(struct folio *folio,
>  	if (referenced_ptes == -1)
>  		return FOLIOREF_KEEP;
>  
> -	if (lru_gen_enabled()) {
> +	if (lru_gen_enabled() && !lru_gen_draining()) {
>  		if (!referenced_ptes)
>  			return FOLIOREF_RECLAIM;

Does this fallback to the legacy reference checking path corrupt MGLRU state?
The legacy path explicitly sets the PG_referenced bit on accessed folios:

	referenced_folio = folio_test_clear_referenced(folio);

	if (referenced_ptes) {
		/* ... */
		folio_set_referenced(folio);

When the state transition is enabling MGLRU, move_folios_to_lru() subsequently
re-adds these folios via lru_gen_add_folio(). However, lru_gen_add_folio() sets
the generation but does not clear LRU_REFS_FLAGS (which includes
PG_referenced). Will this cause folios to enter MGLRU with stale tier bits,
artificially inflating their access counts and distorting future eviction
decisions?

[ ... ]

> @@ -5920,9 +5926,12 @@ static void shrink_lruvec(struct lruvec *lruvec, struct scan_control *sc)
>  	bool proportional_reclaim;
>  	struct blk_plug plug;
>  
> -	if (lru_gen_enabled() && !root_reclaim(sc)) {
> +	if ((lru_gen_enabled() || lru_gen_draining()) && !root_reclaim(sc)) {
>  		lru_gen_shrink_lruvec(lruvec, sc);
> -		return;
> +
> +		if (!lru_gen_draining())
> +			return;
> +
>  	}
>  
>  	get_scan_count(lruvec, sc, nr);

Will this cause severe over-reclaim? The local variable nr_to_reclaim is
initialized from sc->nr_to_reclaim at the start of the function. When
lru_gen_draining() is true, lru_gen_shrink_lruvec() increments
sc->nr_reclaimed if it successfully evicts folios. The code then falls through
to the legacy reclaim loop.

However, the legacy loop uses its own local nr_reclaimed counter (starting at
0) and compares it against the cached nr_to_reclaim. Doesn't this force the
legacy loop to completely ignore MGLRU's progress and redundantly attempt to
reclaim the full target again from the legacy lists?

Additionally, does this joint reclaim design cause the legacy path to
spin on empty lists? Since VMSTAT counters (like NR_INACTIVE_FILE) do not
differentiate between MGLRU and traditional LRU folios, get_scan_count()
generates large scan targets (nr[lru]) even when folios are fully managed
by MGLRU.

The legacy loop then iterates, decrementing nr[lru] by SWAP_CLUSTER_MAX each
time, and calls shrink_list(), which acquires and releases the IRQ-disabled
lruvec->lru_lock. If the legacy lists are physically empty,
isolate_lru_folios() returns 0, and the loop spins without progress until the
massive nr[lru] target hits 0. Can this result in massive lru_lock contention
that actively starves lru_gen_change_state() from acquiring the same lock,
severely prolonging the race window?

[ ... ]

> @@ -6182,10 +6191,13 @@ static void shrink_node(pg_data_t *pgdat, struct scan_control *sc)
>  	struct lruvec *target_lruvec;
>  	bool reclaimable = false;
>  
> -	if (lru_gen_enabled() && root_reclaim(sc)) {
> +	if ((lru_gen_enabled() || lru_gen_draining()) && root_reclaim(sc)) {
>  		memset(&sc->nr, 0, sizeof(sc->nr));
>  		lru_gen_shrink_node(pgdat, sc);
> -		return;
> +
> +		if (!lru_gen_draining())
> +			return;
> +
>  	}
>  
>  	target_lruvec = mem_cgroup_lruvec(sc->target_mem_cgroup, pgdat);

Can this hide MGLRU reclaim progress from vmpressure during global reclaim?
Just after this block, the baseline snapshot is captured:

again:
	memset(&sc->nr, 0, sizeof(sc->nr));

	nr_reclaimed = sc->nr_reclaimed;

Because this is captured after lru_gen_shrink_node() has executed, the
subsequent legacy reclaim path computes its reclaimed count as
nr_node_reclaimed = sc->nr_reclaimed - nr_reclaimed; which completely excludes
any pages reclaimed by MGLRU.

This 0 value is then passed to vmpressure(). If MGLRU satisfies the target but
the legacy lists yield nothing, vmpressure() will calculate a 0% reclaim
efficiency. Could this emit false VMPRESSURE_CRITICAL events, potentially
triggering userspace OOM killers (like oomd) and causing the exact cgroup OOM
kills this patch is intended to resolve?
